title: 5 things to know: Japan unfazed by injuries, Curacao make debut against Germany
original_text:
The first of the four-game days on Sunday was sprinkled with some shocks: Australia beating Turkey and Brazil looking out of sorts in their draw against Morocco, and one Scottish player affectionately known as “Meatball” sending the Tartan Army barmy.
Oh, and Qatar snatched a draw with Switzerland.
If all that action whetted your appetite, then the next 360 minutes of football should feel like more than a satisfying main course.
Curacao, one of the so-called minnow nations, are competing in their first World Cup on Monday and their reward is an opening game against Germany. This is followed by the enticing the Netherlands v Japan match, then Ivory Coast v Ecuador and finally Sweden play Tunisia.
Here are five things you should know heading into day four of the World Cup.
‘Break the shells off’
Japan, who have been tipped to go far, are competing in their eighth straight World Cup and confidence is high after pre-World Cup friendly wins over Brazil and England.

In the last tournament in Qatar, they finished ahead of Spain and Germany (whom they had beaten 2-1) in the group stage before losing to Croatia in the round of 16.
However, the team’s preparation this time has been affected following the abrupt announcement on Thursday that captain Wataru Endo has been forced to withdraw after failing to recover from a foot injury. Endo also said he was retiring from international football.
Head coach Hajime Moriyasu has backed his team to look past the absence of Endo and injured Brighton winger Kaoru Mitoma, who wasn’t fit enough to come to North America.
“When the unexpected happens, we have to make sure we are not upset or shocked too much,” the coach said. “We could be considered a dark horse, but to do that we have to break the shells off what we have been hiding in and try to go to the limit.”
Getting one over on the Dutch, famously known as the best team to never win the World Cup, will be a big statement of intent for Japan when the teams meet at 4am HKT at AT&T Stadium in Dallas.
Baptism of fire
Curacao face a daunting baptism of fire to the World Cup when they take on Germany in Houston in the 1am HKT game.
The World Cup’s smallest country in terms of size and population are managed by Dick Advocaat, the tournament’s oldest coach.
Advocaat said he was looking forward to the test against Julian Nagelsmann, who is the youngest coach at the tournament.
“The fact that we’re playing Germany right at the start is fantastic,” Advocaat said. “We want to advance to the next round. An upset is always possible. And an upset could be all it takes to advance.
“We have a chance. There’s a lot of ambition in this team. And when you have that, you can achieve a lot.”

The debutants, however, will have to find a way to get past Manuel Neuer after the goalkeeper was convinced to come out of retirement by Nagelsmann.
Neuer, who won the last of his 124 international caps in a Euro 2024 quarter-final defeat by Spain, will start in goal.
Nagelsmann brought him out of international retirement after he helped Bayern win the Bundesliga title and reach the Champions League semi-finals this season.
Betting on defence
The Ivory Coast are banking on their solid defence to help them progress at their first World Cup in 12 years. The Ivorians did not concede in 10 qualifiers and beat France 2-1 in a friendly earlier this month.
A reported target of Liverpool and PSG, striker Yan Diomande, will be a key player for the Ivory Coast if they hope to go deep in the tournament.
But first they have to overcome Ecuador, who have not lost since a 1-0 defeat by Brazil in September 2024. The 19-game unbeaten run has filled the team with confidence, especially after they finished second in South American qualifying.
“The past 19 games we have not lost. Our recent history is strong; now we have to show this at the World Cup, the biggest stage of all. We want to put our best foot forward and be demanding of ourselves,” Argentine coach Sebastian Beccacece said.
The match kicks off at 7am HKT.
New sheriff in town
Sweden seem to be embracing the trip to the United States, especially manager Graham Potter who was spotted wearing a cowboy hat to take a training session.
However, the team were lucky to even make it to North America after an abysmal qualifying campaign where they failed to win any games and finished bottom of their group, earning their way into the World Cup on the strength of their performances in the 2024-2025 Nations League.
Sweden captain Victor Lindelof is happy manager Potter has a fit 26-man ‌squad available for selection, meaning he can pick Viktor Gyokeres and Alexander Isak up front.

“I wouldn’t replace ⁠them with anyone,” Lindelof said. “I’m really happy to have the two of them.
“I’m trying to go ⁠through in my mind what other countries, you know, there are [that have a similar partnership], but I think they are two top-class forwards, so it’s amazing to have them on our team.”
Sweden face Tunisia at 10am HKT.
Iran arrive
The team is due to fly into Los Angeles on Sunday from its training camp in Mexico on the eve of its first game.
Iran take on New Zealand in the city on Monday, marking the first time that a World Cup host has hosted a country it is at war with.
But the Iran team has had to leave many of its support staff in Tijuana after the US refused to grant visas to 15 administrative and management staff.
